risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Staff then randomized participants, using 1:1 equal allocation, to receive either routine education only (control arm) or Inform Me after attending routine education (intervention arm), using a computer-generated random number list... Randomization was stratified by site." Comment: This describes a computer-generated random number list with stratification, which is a proper random sequence generation method.

Quote: "Staff then randomized participants, using 1:1 equal allocation, to receive either routine education only (control arm) or Inform Me after attending routine education (intervention arm), using a computer-generated random number list, with individual numbers inserted into sequentially numbered, sealed envelopes concealed until study arm was assigned." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment mechanism that prevents foreknowledge of the next assignment (sequentially numbered, sealed envelopes concealed until assignment), so allocation concealment is low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The trial was single blinded; research team members assessing outcomes (EJG, MWS, MGI) were blinded to assignments to the intervention." Comment: Only outcome assessors were blinded. Participants used either routine education alone or the Inform Me app, and research staff showed intervention users how to use the app and remained present, so participants/personnel delivering the intervention were not blinded. Because the outcomes included knowledge and willingness to accept a kidney offer, which could plausibly be influenced by awareness of allocation and interaction with the app, performance bias is high.

Quote: "The trial was single blinded; research team members assessing outcomes (EJG, MWS, MGI) were blinded to assignments to the intervention." Comment: Outcome assessors were explicitly stated to be blinded. The main outcome was a knowledge test and willingness item completed on paper/electronically by participants, but the paper also states the assessors were blinded; since the key knowledge outcome was self-administered and scored from standardized responses/automatically transmitted data, knowledge assessment was unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation.

Quote: "Our analysis was based on the intention-to-treat principle and included all persons who were randomized regardless of whether they completed the posttest assessments. ... Because they were missing at random, we imputed them using multiple imputation based on a Bayesian iterative Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method ..." Comment: The paper explicitly reports ITT analysis including randomized participants, tests for missingness, and uses multiple imputation for missing outcome data. Although 1 person did not receive the intervention and 17 did not complete it, the authors state that noncompleters were similar demographically to completers and the imputed and complete-case results were very similar, so attrition is unlikely to have biased the outcome.
